title: Tighten a Loose Screw
summary: Find the right screwdriver, fit it to the screw head, and tighten loose screws and small bolts around your home.
learnerPromise: Use a screwdriver to tighten loose screws and small bolts.
whyItMatters: Loose screws make furniture wobbly, cabinet doors saggy, and hinges squeaky. Knowing how to tighten them is a simple fix that makes things feel solid and safe again.
realLifeUses: Fix a wobbly chair or table
Tighten a cabinet door that hangs crooked
youWillLearn: How to match a screwdriver type to a screw head
How to tell if a screw is a flathead or Phillips
How to tighten firmly without stripping the screw
When to stop tightening so you do not damage the material
miniChallenge: Find one loose screw in your home and tighten it with the right screwdriver.
steps: Look around your home for one item with a loose screw — a hinge, handle, or piece of furniture.
Look at the screw head and choose a flathead or Phillips screwdriver that fits snugly into the grooves.
Insert the screwdriver tip fully into the screw head so it sits flush.
Turn the screwdriver clockwise to tighten until the screw feels firm.
Test the item to see if it feels more stable, and stop when it does — do not overtighten.
completionCriteria: The item feels more stable than before
commonProblems: Screwdriver keeps slipping — the size or type does not match; try a different one
Screw turns but does not tighten — the hole may be stripped; try a slightly larger screw
Not sure which screwdriver to use — compare the tip shape to the screw head grooves
tips: Apply firm, steady pressure while turning to keep the screwdriver seated
If a screw is hard to reach, a magnetic screwdriver can help hold the screw in place
Keep a small set of screwdrivers in a handy spot for quick fixes
helpBoundary: Ask for help if the screw keeps spinning or the material is damaged.
